record_office_terminology.land_records: Register of Deeds (deeds, mortgages, liens, plats -- one office per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Court (wills, estates, guardianships, adoptions -- Nebraska unified court system)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Court / County Clerk (marriage licenses; county-level records)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1904 (state registration of births and deaths began; compliance improved after 1910; marriages registered at county level from county formation, state registration from 1909). Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services holds state copies.

burned_county_authority: Nebraska has no significant burned counties. Record survival is generally excellent across all 93 counties. History Nebraska (Nebraska State Historical Society) in Lincoln serves as the central repository for state records, microfilmed county records, and manuscript collections.
administrative_notes: Nebraska has 93 counties, organized between 1854 and 1909 (Garden County was the last formed). Nebraska Territory was established by the Kansas-Nebraska Act of 1854; statehood was achieved on March 1, 1867 (37th state). Nebraska is a public land state -- all original land transfers from federal ownership are documented through the General Land Office (patents searchable at glorecords.blm.gov). The Homestead Act of 1862 was critically important -- the first homestead claim under the act was filed in Gage County (Daniel Freeman, January 1, 1863). The Timber Culture Act (1873) and the Kinkaid Act (1904, allowing 640-acre claims in the Sandhills) also generated significant land records. Nebraska's population includes strong German, Czech/Bohemian, Swedish, Danish, Polish, and Irish immigrant communities, each producing rich church and fraternal records. Native American tribes historically present include the Omaha, Ponca, Pawnee, Lakota/Sioux, and Winnebago (Ho-Chunk), with reservations in Thurston and Knox counties. The Oregon Trail, California Trail, and Mormon Trail all crossed Nebraska along the Platte Valley. The Union Pacific Railroad (transcontinental, completed 1869) followed the Platte Valley and profoundly shaped settlement patterns. History Nebraska (NSHS) in Lincoln is the central repository. The Nebraska State Genealogical Society provides statewide research support. Register of Deeds offices hold land records; County Courts handle probate, marriage, and guardianship records.

core_repositories.description: Nebraska's central historical repository and the single most important resource for genealogical research in Nebraska. Holdings include microfilmed county records (deeds, probate, marriages, court records), vital records indexes, military records, manuscript collections, photographs, maps, newspapers on microfilm, territorial-era government records, and the Nebraska State Archives. Research room open to the public in Lincoln.

Statewide genealogical society providing research support, publications, and educational programs. Publishes the Nebraska Ancestree journal. Maintains surname files, county research guides, and a growing digital collection. County chapters provide local support.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Nebraska-Lincoln Archives and Special Collections
core_repositories.description: Manuscript collections, Nebraska authors (including Willa Cather materials), agricultural records, and regional history. The Center for Great Plains Studies holds interdisciplinary collections on Plains settlement and culture.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive indexed and browsable collections including Nebraska county records (deeds, probate, marriages, court records), vital records, church records, cemetery records, and military records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at Nebraska county courthouses and at History Nebraska. Key collections include Nebraska marriages, probate records, and land records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Nebraska's county record system, homestead research, immigration patterns, and ethnic community records.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Nebraska newspapers. Nebraska had hundreds of newspapers -- county seats and small towns each typically had at least one weekly paper. German-language, Czech-language, and Scandinavian-language newspapers were common. Obituaries, legal notices, homestead proof notices, and community news are essential for Nebraska research.

History Nebraska holds the most extensive collection of Nebraska newspapers on microfilm. Coverage includes territorial-era papers (1854-1867), county weeklies, city dailies (Omaha World-Herald, Lincoln Star), and ethnic-language newspapers. Nebraska Newspapers digitization project expanding access.

military_records.source_name: Nebraska Military Records -- History Nebraska / NARA
military_records.description: Nebraska Adjutant General records, Civil War rosters (Nebraska contributed ~3,300 Union troops from a small territorial population), Indian Wars service, Spanish-American War, WWI and WWII records. History Nebraska holds military service records, veterans' bonus files, and Grand Army of the Republic (GAR) post records. Fort records (Kearny, McPherson, Robinson, Sidney, Niobrara) at NARA.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Nebraska
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Nebraska territorial soldiers. Nebraska Territory contributed approximately 3,300 men to the Union army despite its small population, organized into the 1st Nebraska Infantry and 1st Nebraska Cavalry.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM GLO -- Nebraska Federal Land Patents
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
land_and_property.description: Nebraska is a public land state. All original transfers of land from federal to private ownership are documented in GLO patents. Nebraska land was surveyed under the rectangular survey system (township, range, section). Homestead entries, pre-emption claims, timber culture entries, Kinkaid Act claims (640-acre homesteads in the Sandhills), cash sales, military warrants, and railroad land grants. Searchable online by name and location.

land_and_property.source_name: Homestead National Historical Park -- Research Resources
land_and_property.description: The Homestead National Historical Park in Beatrice (Gage County) preserves the site of the first homestead claim (Daniel Freeman, January 1, 1863). Research resources include homestead documentation, land office records, and educational materials about the Homestead Act and its impact on Nebraska settlement.

vital_records.source_name: Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services -- Vital Records
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from 1904 (registration compliance improved after 1910). Marriage records at county level from county formation; state registration from 1909. Divorce records through district courts. For pre-1904 events, researchers must use church records, cemetery records, newspaper notices, and county records.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Various -- History Nebraska, denominational archives, FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: Nebraska's diverse immigrant populations produced rich church records. German Lutheran (Concordia University Archives, Seward), German and Irish Catholic (Archdiocese of Omaha, Diocese of Lincoln, Diocese of Grand Island), Czech Catholic and ZCBJ fraternal records, Swedish Lutheran (Augustana Synod), Danish Lutheran (Dana College archives), Polish Catholic, and Methodist records. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Nebraska church records.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Nebraska. Coverage varies by county. Nebraska State Genealogical Society and county historical societies have published cemetery transcription volumes. Township, church, and family cemeteries are common in rural Nebraska.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing Nebraska Ancestree journal. County chapters throughout the state. Surname files, cemetery transcriptions, county research guides, and educational programs.

Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 93 Nebraska counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, county histories, surname queries, and research guides. Quality and depth varies by county.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Nebraska has several extinct counties from the territorial period. L'Eau Qui Court County (1854-1867) covered northeastern Nebraska before being divided into Knox, Antelope, and other counties. Loup County (original, 1855) was an early precursor to Platte County. Blackbird County (1889) was renamed Thurston County in 1889. Shorter County (1855, renamed Colfax 1862). Jones County (1856, renamed Jefferson 1856). Clay County (original) boundaries were different from the modern county. Many county boundaries shifted during the territorial period (1854-1867) and the rapid organization period (1867-1890). Researchers tracing families before 1880 should be aware of these boundary changes and check predecessor counties for records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Nebraska Naturalization Records -- District Courts / NARA
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: History Nebraska / NARA Kansas City / County District Courts
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records filed in Nebraska district courts from the territorial period. Declarations of intention and petitions for naturalization. Nebraska attracted massive immigration -- German, Czech/Bohemian, Swedish, Danish, Polish, Irish, and German-Russian (Volga German) immigrants. Federal court naturalizations at NARA Kansas City. History Nebraska holds some naturalization indexes. FamilySearch has microfilmed many county naturalization records.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: American Historical Society of Germans from Russia (AHSGR)
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Headquartered in Lincoln, AHSGR is the leading repository for German-Russian (Volga German and Black Sea German) genealogical research. Holdings include family files, village histories, church records, immigration records, and cemetery transcriptions for German-Russian communities in Nebraska and across the Great Plains. Essential for researching the significant German-Russian population in Adams, Scotts Bluff, and other Nebraska counties.

native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: NARA Kansas City / Bureau of Indian Affairs / Tribal Governments
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Records of the Omaha and Winnebago (Ho-Chunk) reservations in Thurston County. BIA agency records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, annuity rolls, school records (Omaha Mission School, Genoa Indian School), and agency correspondence. The Winnebago were relocated from Minnesota in 1863. Tribal governments may have additional records with restricted access.

native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: NARA Kansas City / NARA Fort Worth (Oklahoma records)
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Records of the Pawnee tribe, who inhabited central Nebraska (Loup and Platte valleys) until their removal to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) in the 1870s. Pawnee Agency records at NARA include census rolls, agency correspondence, treaty records, and allotment records. The former Pawnee Reservation became Nance County (1879). Researchers should check both NARA Kansas City and NARA Fort Worth for Pawnee records.

native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: NARA Kansas City / Bureau of Indian Affairs
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Records of the Santee Sioux (Dakota) Reservation in Knox County. The Santee Dakota were relocated from Minnesota after the 1862 conflict. BIA agency records include census rolls, allotment records, school records (Santee Normal Training School), and agency correspondence. NARA Kansas City holds the primary collections.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Ponca Tribe Records and Standing Bear Documentation
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Records of the Ponca tribe, historically in northeast Nebraska (Dixon County area). The Ponca were forcibly removed to Indian Territory in 1877. Chief Standing Bear's landmark 1879 court case (Standing Bear v. Crook) in Omaha established that Native Americans are 'persons' under the law. Ponca Agency records, removal records, and court case documentation at NARA and History Nebraska.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Nebraska has no burned counties. Record survival across all 93 counties is generally excellent. Nebraska's counties were organized during a relatively short period (1854-1909) and the state experienced no significant courthouse fires or disasters that destroyed county records. History Nebraska holds microfilm copies of many county records as backup. FamilySearch also microfilmed extensively at Nebraska county offices. Western Sandhills counties (organized in the 1880s-1900s) have smaller record volumes due to sparse population rather than record loss.
